_comment: Species profiles. Scoring: same engine as environments — factors with curves/tables and weights. 'season' is a Jan..Dec presence multiplier (0-1) for Sydney, scored as its own factor. 'environments' are affinity multipliers; the species' final score in an environment = affinity x (env_blend x environment_score + (1 - env_blend) x species_score), and the best environment is surfaced. 'window' = local hours the species is realistically targeted (mulloway runs past midnight). CONTENT NOTE: baits/rigs/seasonality drafted from published Australian angling knowledge + owner experience — owner verifies against NSW DPI before publishing; size/bag limits are deliberately linked out, never republished.

factors.justification: The definitive after-dark predator. Daytime mulloway are the exception, not the plan.
The classic Sydney mulloway trigger: a decent fresh pushing colour and bait down the system. Full floods are too much.
Run-out through deep estuary holes and bridge lines is the standard ambush setup.
The hour around the change — especially the top of the tide at night — is the storied jewie window.
Caught year-round in Sydney; estuary fishing traditionally peaks through winter.
Bigger tides push more bait through the holes.
LOW EVIDENCE, but the new/full bias is unusually persistent in mulloway lore, so it carries slightly more weight here than elsewhere — still small and disclosed.
Falling glass before a change is a repeated theme in mulloway captures.
Tolerant of cool water — part of why they headline winter.

lures: 5–7" soft plastic paddle tails worked slow and deep
rigs: Running sinker straight to a 6/0–8/0 circle or suicide hook (estuary/harbour)
Star sinker paternoster or running rig with 7/0+ for the beach
guide.leader: 40–60 lb fluorocarbon or hard mono, ~1 m
guide.rod: 9–10'6" surf rod off the sand; 7' 6–10 kg rod in the estuary
guide.season_notes: Year-round with a winter estuary peak; the days after decent rain are the days to go.
guide.habitat: Deep estuary holes and bridge pylons on the run-out after rain; beach gutters well after dark; washy headlands into the night. Think depth, current and darkness.
guide.safety_note: Night sessions: tell someone where you are, and never fish rock ledges alone in the dark.

factors.justification: The single strongest kingfish variable in Sydney: the bite switches on with warm EAC water, reliably from ~19 °C.
October–May is the Sydney season, peaking in high summer; winter fish exist but are a specialist pursuit.
First light at the markers is the appointment. They keep feeding through the day more than most inshore species.
Current past vertical structure is what lines kings up to be caught.
The change at a marker or ledge is a known trigger window.
Mostly a boat-handling limit; kings themselves don't mind chop.
General settled-weather correlation.
More range, more current at the structure.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

guide.difficulty: Hard — strong, dirty fighters near structure
rigs: Live bait on a 5/0–7/0 circle, lightly weighted or under a balloon/float
guide.rod: 7' PE3–5 spin outfit; heavier from the stones
guide.season_notes: Warm months (Oct–May). Watch the water temp line on this site — over 19 °C, start planning.
guide.habitat: Channel markers, moorings, wharf pylons and harbour points on the tide change; offshore reefs and FADs by boat. Always near structure, always in current.
guide.safety_note: Lock the drag and expect to get reefed — fish heavier than feels polite.

factors.justification: Bream hold on structure and eat what the run brings past — the run-out along a rock wall or oyster lease is the classic drift.
Low light lowers their notorious caution; lit wharves fish well into the night.
A modest fresh gets bream feeding on washed-in food; a flood pushes them down the system.
Feeding tempo rises with water temperature.
Genuinely year-round in Sydney — one of the reasons they're the ideal beginner target — with a warm-month edge and a winter surf-beach run.
The species behind most 'barometer' folklore — the settled-high correlation is strongest in shallow bream water.
Sharp swings shut shallow fish down first.
More water over the leases and flats to feed on.
The last of the run-out concentrates fish at drop-offs.
Comfort and casting accuracy for light-line work.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

guide.difficulty: Easy to catch, hard to master
lures: Surface poppers over flats in summer
rigs: Light running sinker (or no weight at all) to a size 2–1/0 hook — the less weight the better
guide.season_notes: All year. The most reliable 'just want to catch something' target on this list.
guide.habitat: Pontoons, rock walls, wharf pylons, oyster leases and moored boats on the run-out; surf gutters in winter. If it's structure with current, there's a bream on it.

factors.justification: The most light-gated species here: tailor sessions are dawn and dusk appointments, almost to the minute.
Autumn–winter is the Sydney beach run; summer fish are smaller and scattered.
Tailor are white-water hunters — they want turbulence and foam, not calm.
A working onshore builds the wash they hunt in.
Tide movement through the gutters moves the bait schools they follow.
A cooler-water species — part of why they own autumn and winter.
Stronger flushes through the gutters.
General settled/gentle-fall correlation.
Minor factor for a fish that likes rough edges anyway.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

baits: WA pilchards on ganged hooks (the institution)
rigs: 3–4 x 4/0 ganged hooks under a star or ball sinker, rigged so the pilchard swims straight
guide.leader: 25–40 lb hard mono — their teeth destroy light leader
guide.rod: 9–10' surf rod; 7–8' spin rod off the rocks
guide.season_notes: Autumn and winter, first and last light. Set an alarm; the window is real and it is short.
guide.habitat: Beach gutters with white water at dawn/dusk; washy headlands. Follow the birds — working terns mean bait, and bait means tailor.
guide.safety_note: Fingers out of mouths — tailor bite back.

factors.justification: Schools sit in current lines and deep holes, feeding on what the tide delivers.
The cool-season counterpart to kingfish in the harbour — winter is trevally time.
A genuinely cool-water preference, opposite sign to the kingfish curve — a clean demonstration of why species get their own profiles.
Better in low light but takeable all day in deep water, especially with berley.
The change stacks the school up in the hole.
Tolerant of a little colour; a flood pushes them out to cleaner water.
Settled-weather correlation.
As elsewhere.
Comfort and berley-trail control.
More current through the holes.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

baits: Bread flesh (with a bread berley trail)
rigs: Light running sinker or unweighted bait drifted down a berley trail, size 4–1 hook
guide.season_notes: The cooler months' consolation prize that fights better than it should. Winter harbour staple.
guide.habitat: Deep harbour holes, wharf shadows and current lines on the tide change; keep a berley trail going and they'll find you.

factors.justification: Squid are sight hunters — water clarity is everything, and recent rain is the best free proxy for it. The sharpest negative-rain curve on the site, deliberately opposite to mulloway.
Dawn/dusk peaks; lit wharves make night sessions productive.
Swell stirs the weed beds and kills visibility along ocean-influenced edges.
Year-round, with the clear, cool water of winter the quiet peak.
Wind chop ruins both visibility and jig control.
Gentle flow beats ripping current — squid hover over the weed rather than fight the tide.
Broad tolerance; mild preference for the middle of the range.
Settled weather = clear water, mostly an indirect effect.
As elsewhere.
Mild — enough movement to bring feed over the beds without blowing visibility.
LOW EVIDENCE. Dark nights concentrate squid under artificial lights, which nudges this slightly above the default folklore weight.

baits: Optional: baited spike jig with a whole yakka under a float
lures: Size 2.5–3.5 squid jigs; naturals in bright sun, glow/UV at dawn, dusk and night
rigs: Jig tied direct to the leader. Cast past the weed edge, let it sink, two slow whips of the rod, long pause — they take on the pause
guide.rod: Any light 7' spin rod works; a dedicated egi rod is a luxury, not a requirement
guide.season_notes: All year. Winter's clear water is quietly the best of it. Also the best fresh bait you can own — catch two, fish one, keep one for the kingfish.
guide.habitat: Weed beds and kelp-fringed edges in clear harbour water; wharf lights after dark. If you can see the bottom, you're in squid country.
guide.safety_note: Hold them head-away for the first ten seconds unless you want the ink.

factors.justification: Around all day; thicker around lights at night. The flattest curve on the site because berley matters more than any condition.
Comfort and keeping a berley trail tight.
Some flow carries the berley; too much scatters it.
Dirty water thins the schools at the wharves.
Genuinely year-round — the reason they're the live-bait currency of Sydney.
Broad tolerance.
Minor.
Minor.
Minor.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

baits: Tiny cubes of pilchard, prawn or bread on the hook
lures: Sabiki bait jigs — drop, jiggle, wind up two at a time
rigs: Size 8–12 long-shank under a small float or lightly weighted; or a sabiki rig with a small sinker on the bottom
guide.rod: Anything light; a $30 combo is genuinely fine
guide.season_notes: All year, every wharf. The gateway fish — and the live bait that catches the kingfish and mulloway above.
guide.habitat: Wharf pylons and moored-boat shadows in the harbour and lower estuary. Start a bread berley trail and they appear in minutes.
guide.safety_note: Keeping them alive: an aerated bucket, change the water often, don't overcrowd it.
